result.summary: Solved "Diameter of Binary Tree" in Java 17. Optimal single post-order DFS computing edge-heights (null=-1, leaf=0) and tracking max bent path (leftHeight+rightHeight+2); O(n) time, O(h) space, no overflow risk since diameter <= n-1 fits in int. Wrote Solution.java (with stdin level-order harness) and Main.java (12-case harness). Compiled with /opt/homebrew/opt/openjdk@17/bin/javac *.java and ran Main — all 12 cases PASS: prompt example (3), empty (0), single (0), two nodes (1), left/right skewed chains, path-NOT-through-root (4), perfect tree height 3 (6), unequal arms (5), 10^4 skewed-chain stress (9999, no stack overflow), 16383-node perfect tree (26), level-order parse. Stdin checks confirm "1 2 3 null 4 5"->4, empty->0, "7"->0. Full 5-step assessment_java response written to /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora_java/workspace/java-coding-treediameter-001/_response.md; presented code matches verified Solution.java.
result.title: Diameter of Binary Tree

findings.problem: The height() function is recursive, so recursion depth equals tree height. At the stated max constraint (n=10^4, fully skewed) it works fine on the default JVM stack (verified: diameter=9999, no overflow), but it overflows with a constrained stack (verified: StackOverflowError under -Xss256k). Not a correctness failure under normal execution given the small constraint, but it is the only structural soft spot.
findings.fix: Optional hardening for larger constraints: convert the post-order traversal to an explicit stack (iterative) or note the O(n) stack-depth assumption. No change needed for n<=10^4.

result.summary: Solved LeetCode 84 Largest Rectangle in Histogram in Java 17. Wrote and verified Solution.java (monotonic increasing stack of indices, O(n) time / O(n) space, with a long accumulator to avoid the 10^9 max-area overflow near int's ceiling) plus a brute-force O(n^2) oracle. Main.java harness covers the example, single bar, single zero, empty, all-equal, increasing, decreasing, zero-split walls, two bars, valley, all-zeros, and a 100K-bar max-area overflow guard, plus a 5000-case random brute-vs-optimal stress and a 100K performance run — ALL TESTS PASSED. Wrote the full 11-section interview_java response to _response.md: timing box, Sections 1-5 speakable opener (constraints table, ASCII histogram + before/after stack insight, monotonic-stack pattern tag, brute/D&C/stack landscape, deep-dive trace), then verified code (6a brute + 6b optimal), harder valley walkthrough, amortized O(n) complexity explanation, optimality lower-bound, edge cases/pitfalls (width off-by-one, flush, store indices), follow-ups, and an If-You-Get-Stuck section. Presented code matches the verified Solution.java exactly.
result.title: Largest Rectangle in Histogram — Java solution + full interview walkthrough

findings.problem: The final `return (int) maxArea;` cast in Solution.java largestRectangleArea (line 31) truncates silently if the area exceeds Integer.MAX_VALUE. Within the stated constraints (n <= 10^5, height <= 10^4) the max area is exactly 10^9, which fits in int, so this is NOT a bug for the problem as specified. I verified that an out-of-constraint input (n=2*10^5, h=2*10^4 -> 4*10^9) returns a negative -294967296. The response correctly notes area is 'CLOSE' to the int ceiling and accumulates in long to avoid intermediate overflow, which is the right call; only the final return narrows. No action required given constraints.
findings.fix: Optional defensive hardening only: return the long or document that the return relies on the constraint area <= 10^9 < int max. Not needed for the stated problem.

findings.problem: Presentation vs file structure: Solution.java bundles both the optimal method (largestRectangleArea) and the brute-force oracle (largestRectangleAreaBrute) in one class, while _response.md Section 6a presents the brute force as a separate standalone `public class Solution` and 6b the optimal. The optimal code in 6b matches Solution.java's method verbatim, and this split is the expected/intended presentation (6a safety net, 6b headline), so it is acceptable, not a defect.
findings.fix: No change needed. If strict file-to-response parity is desired, the brute oracle method could be mentioned as a test-harness helper.
